findings.specific_passage: Push back on the 7-day deadline. At this level, especially for a founding engineer role, a 10-14 day decision window is completely normal, and you can ask for it. "I'm taking this seriously enough that I want to do the right diligence. Can we extend the decision window by a week?" If they say no, that itself is information — if they're refusing to give a senior candidate a reasonable due diligence window, they're either desperate for someone or bad at managing their own process, neither of which is good news.
findings.challenge_statement: Which approval, precedent, or prerequisite check is being skipped because the team is treating the clock as proof?
findings.next_move: Before committing, state the long-term rule that should still govern the move and hold that line for one full review cycle.
major_tensions: If speed is becoming the reason to suspend scrutiny, assume stress is narrowing thought and require a deliberate pause before action.
findings.intervention_hint: Before committing, state the long-term rule that should still govern the move and hold that line for one full review cycle.

If they say no, that itself is information — if they're refusing to give a senior candidate a reasonable due diligence window, they're either desperate for someone or bad at managing their own process, neither of which is good news.

chunks.text: Decision paralysis can keep the team comparing endless alternatives instead of acting on a reversible test.

This model depends on trade-offs (foundation-for), which is not active in the answer. Opportunity cost is the economic quantification of trade-offs. Understanding that resources are scarce and forces constantly conflict (trade-offs) precedes the ability to quantify what specific value is lost when choosing one path over another (opportunity cost).
Before approval: name the single most valuable thing this decision makes impossible or significantly harder for the next 12 months.
Anchoring: Opportunity-cost conflicts with anchoring when the comparison is pinned to the wrong baseline instead of the best real alternative.
Upgrade simple pro-con thinking into a comparison that includes the implicit costs of the best foregone option.

anchors.evidence_quote: A answers a question you probably already know the answer to. B answers one you genuinely don't know. From an information-value standpoint, B is the more interesting move.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer frames Option A as lower-leverage (known info) versus B's higher-leverage unique learning about building from zero, using opportunity-cost comparison to prioritize information value.

chunks.text: Select when: A negotiation or partnership sounds like a price or policy dispute on the surface, but the real issue may be leverage, outside options, or commitment costs. | Danger when: The true constraint is exogenous or non-negotiable, so treating the situation as a power contest would distract from the actual bottleneck. | Reasoning: systems, diagnostic
Teams can treat verbal toughness as leverage even when the walk-away is not credible and the fallback option is worse than the current deal.
A signed deal can be mistaken for durable control even though the next commitment hands timing, data, or switching-cost leverage to the counterparty.
What would make our walk-away or delay threat binding rather than rhetorical?
Separate stated willingness from credible walk-away by checking enforcement cost, internal approval, and operational fallback.

anchors.evidence_quote: Push back on the 7-day deadline. At this level, especially for a founding engineer role, a 10-14 day decision window is completely normal, and you can ask for it. "I'm taking this seriously enough that I want to do the right diligence. Can we extend the decision window by a week?" If they say no, that itself is information
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer identifies the deadline as a leverage play in negotiation and advises testing the startup's outside options/commitment by pushing back, applying power-dynamics to reveal the true constraint.

chunks.text: Anti-sunk-cost logic becomes reckless when it ignores switching costs, learning value, or real upside that still remains.
Ask whether the team would willingly recommit today before treating continued exposure as rational.
Would we start again today from current facts?
What future upside actually remains if we ignore what is already gone?
Commitment Bias: Commitment-bias fuels sunk-cost-fallacy by making prior investment feel like a reason to continue exposure rather than a reason to reevaluate.
chunks.affinity_rationale: Canonical describes commitment/consistency as the active psychological mechanism that forces persistence in sunk cost behavior
chunks.activation_condition: When the drive for consistency with prior decisions forces continued investment despite negative evidence

anchors.evidence_quote: You've been mentally checked out of your current job for a year. If you stay, that doesn't go away — in fact it gets worse, because now you've passed on two real opportunities and you'll resent yourself
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer warns against persisting in the current job due to prior time investment (a year checked out) and future resentment from passing opportunities, enforcing anti-sunk-cost logic by prioritizing forward-looking value.

chunks.text: Reflection can become self-soothing introspection that leaves the actual decision process unchanged.
How am I thinking about this, and what might that process be missing?
Deliberately monitor the thinking process, not just the output, while the work is still adjustable.
Confirmation Bias: Confirmation-bias weakens meta-cognitive-reflection by making the self-audit protect the current story instead of revising it.
chunks.affinity_rationale: MCR 'struggles against the deeply ingrained human instinct to dismiss new, disconfirming information and instead seek data that validates existing beliefs' — directly opposes the open inquiry MCR demands.
chunks.activation_condition: When self-monitoring is used to rationalize existing beliefs rather than genuinely examining whether the reasoning process is producing accurate rather than comfortable conclusions

chunks.text: Confirmation Bias: Meta-cognitive-reflection conflicts with confirmation-bias when process monitoring is replaced by belief-protecting self-explanation.

anchors.evidence_quote: "When I'm not being rational." You're framing the pull toward B as the irrational option, which suggests you've already decided the "rational" answer is A and you're asking me to confirm it.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer inspects the user's own thinking process—identifying biased framing of options as rational/irrational—to uncover the hidden career-shape question, applying meta-cognitive reflection as the mechanism.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Sunk-cost-fallacy conflicts with commitment-bias when prior investment is used to justify continued exposure.
expansions.why_relevant: Sunk-cost-fallacy conflicts with commitment-bias when prior investment is used to justify continued exposure.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Confirmation-bias locks sunk-cost-fallacy in place by filtering for evidence that keeps the original investment looking defensible.
expansions.why_relevant: Confirmation-bias locks sunk-cost-fallacy in place by filtering for evidence that keeps the original investment looking defensible.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Canonical states confirmation bias locks individuals into failing paths by filtering for supporting information
expansions.activation_condition: When selective information filtering supports original investment decisions while discounting disconfirming evidence

expansions.substrate_chunk: Confirmation-bias weakens meta-cognitive-reflection by making the self-audit protect the current story instead of revising it.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Meta-cognitive-reflection conflicts with emotional-intelligence when introspective analysis delays timely relational response.
expansions.why_relevant: Meta-cognitive-reflection conflicts with emotional-intelligence when introspective analysis delays timely relational response.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Confirmation-bias distorts power-dynamics by making teams see the leverage structure they want rather than the outside option they actually have.
expansions.why_relevant: Confirmation-bias distorts power-dynamics by making teams see the leverage structure they want rather than the outside option they actually have.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Directly distorts the core leverage analysis — makes teams see the leverage structure they want rather than the outside option evidence actually supports.
expansions.activation_condition: When assessing relative bargaining position and the team's evidence for its outside option has not been independently tested

expansions.substrate_chunk: Power-dynamics conflicts with confirmation-bias when a team keeps acting as if its outside option is stronger than the evidence supports.
expansions.why_relevant: Power-dynamics conflicts with confirmation-bias when a team keeps acting as if its outside option is stronger than the evidence supports.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Opportunity-cost counters power-dynamics tunnel vision by asking whether winning the negotiation is even worth pursuing relative to better alternatives.
expansions.why_relevant: Opportunity-cost counters power-dynamics tunnel vision by asking whether winning the negotiation is even worth pursuing relative to better alternatives.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Broader perspective correction — asks whether winning the negotiation is worth pursuing relative to better alternatives, rather than targeting the leverage analysis itself.
expansions.activation_condition: When teams are deeply invested in winning a specific negotiation without comparing to the value of their next-best alternative

expansions.substrate_chunk: Opportunity-cost conflicts with anchoring when the comparison is pinned to the wrong baseline instead of the best real alternative.
expansions.why_relevant: Opportunity-cost conflicts with anchoring when the comparison is pinned to the wrong baseline instead of the best real alternative.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Confirmation-bias obstructs opportunity-cost by making an unfavored alternative hard to assess objectively.
expansions.why_relevant: Confirmation-bias obstructs opportunity-cost by making an unfavored alternative hard to assess objectively.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Confirmation bias 'obstructs the objective assessment of opportunity cost' by making one reluctant to recognize the potential benefits of unfavored alternatives.
expansions.activation_condition: When the assessment of foregone alternatives is biased by preference for the current option rather than honest evaluation of what is being sacrificed

expansions.substrate_chunk: Opportunity-cost conflicts with status-quo-bias when default continuation hides the best foregone alternative.
expansions.why_relevant: Opportunity-cost conflicts with status-quo-bias when default continuation hides the best foregone alternative.

failure_hints.text: Anti-sunk-cost logic becomes reckless when it ignores switching costs, learning value, or real upside that still remains.
The model fails when history is treated as a reason to continue instead of as an irrecoverable fact that should be ignored for forward choice.
Reflection can become self-soothing introspection that leaves the actual decision process unchanged.
The model fails when motion and effort continue without any monitoring of whether the current strategy is working.
A signed deal can be mistaken for durable control even though the next commitment hands timing, data, or switching-cost leverage to the counterparty.
Teams can treat verbal toughness as leverage even when the walk-away is not credible and the fallback option is worse than the current deal.
A request can look cheap in isolation while still stealing the same launch window, engineering time, or management attention from the highest-value move.
Decision paralysis can keep the team comparing endless alternatives instead of acting on a reversible test.

heuristic_hints.text: Ask whether the team would willingly recommit today before treating continued exposure as rational.
Reset the choice from current facts and future value instead of from prior investment.
Deliberately monitor the thinking process, not just the output, while the work is still adjustable.
Translate reflection into a corrected next move so the loop changes behavior instead of just self-description.
Map outside options before discussing terms because fallback quality is part of the real price floor.
Separate stated willingness from credible walk-away by checking enforcement cost, internal approval, and operational fallback.
Force every recommendation to answer compared to what before calling it attractive.
Upgrade simple pro-con thinking into a comparison that includes the implicit costs of the best foregone option.

premortem_hints.text: Would we start again today from current facts?
What future upside actually remains if we ignore what is already gone?
How am I thinking about this, and what might that process be missing?
What sign would show that this reflection improved the next move rather than just my feeling of control?
At what milestone does our cost to reverse become higher than the counterparty's cost to lose us?
What would make our walk-away or delay threat binding rather than rhetorical?
Before approval: name the single most valuable thing this decision makes impossible or significantly harder for the next 12 months.
Which comparison in this plan is pretending to be irreversible even though we could run it as a bounded test first, and what learning are we giving up by waiting for certainty before acting?

select_when: A team is persisting mainly because of prior investment, history, or identity rather than because the future economics still justify recommitment.
The real decision is whether we would start again today from current facts, not whether we can emotionally justify past effort.
A project, relationship, or investment needs to be reevaluated from prospective value instead of from already-incurred cost.
The next useful move is to separate what is irrecoverable from what still has live future upside.
danger_when: Anti-sunk-cost logic is being used as cover for reckless abandonment without evaluating learning value, switching costs, or remaining upside.
The team already understands the sunk cost clearly and now mainly needs execution on the chosen exit or recommitment path.

select_when: The thinker needs to inspect how they are thinking, not just what they are concluding, because the process itself may be the constraint.
Planning, learning, or decision quality would improve if the team deliberately monitored assumptions, progress, and blind spots.
The work is moving, but without enough self-audit to notice drift, confusion, or shallow understanding.
A pause for internalized teacher-like review could improve the next move more than more raw effort.
danger_when: Reflection is becoming self-soothing, journaling theater, or introspective overprocessing instead of improving the next decision.
The situation is already clear and the team needs deliberate action, repetition, or execution more than another layer of process self-observation.

select_when: A negotiation or partnership sounds like a price or policy dispute on the surface, but the real issue may be leverage, outside options, or commitment costs.
The team needs to know who can actually wait, walk away, redirect demand, or absorb reversal costs before accepting the stated framing of the deal.
A commitment, integration, or dependency may invert leverage over time even if the current terms look favorable at signature.
The next move should map fallback lanes, enforcement credibility, and the point where lock-in changes what is actually negotiable.
danger_when: The true constraint is exogenous or non-negotiable, so treating the situation as a power contest would distract from the actual bottleneck.
The team is already over-reading symbolic status or tone as leverage and needs a factual capability or execution review instead.

select_when: A team wants to add one more initiative even though the same people, budget, or launch window are needed by a higher-leverage move.
A default renewal, inherited project, or existing channel is being treated as free because the next-best use of the same resources has not been named.
Resources are scarce and each yes displaces a real alternative in capital, attention, headcount, or calendar time.
Trade-offs span more than money and the decision should price lost optionality, learning, morale, or trust rather than only explicit cash cost.
danger_when: The option set is still poorly framed, so comparison would create false precision around the wrong alternatives or objective function.
The analysis is loading the comparison with unrealistic alternatives that are not genuinely available, fundable, or executable.

frame_elements.element_text: The decision is among exactly three predefined job options: FAANG staff+, Series B founding engineer, or stay at current company.
frame_elements.evidence_quote: deciding among three job offers (FAANG staff+, Series B founding engineer, stay)
frame_elements.fragility_signal: Other viable paths exist, such as negotiating promotion at current company, seeking staff roles at non-FAANG big tech, independent consulting, or sabbatical to explore building own startup.

frame_elements.element_text: No clear path to staff at current company due to structural issues, not personal performance.
frame_elements.evidence_quote: No clear path to staff at current company (explicit feedback, structural)
frame_elements.fragility_signal: Re-reading performance reviews or seeking second opinions reveals actionable feedback on leadership growth, making internal promotion viable.

reframings.reframed_question: What if, instead of choosing among FAANG staff+, Series B founding engineer, or staying, you negotiated a staff promotion at your current FAANG company before the 7-day deadline?
reframings.what_opens: Explores leveraging 12 years FAANG experience and explicit structural feedback to create an internal path to staff, potentially avoiding external moves.

reframings.reframed_question: Could seeking a second opinion on your 'meets expectations' leadership reviews from a mentor or external coach reveal actionable steps to unlock a staff path at your current company?
reframings.what_opens: Reopens the dropped thread on performance reviews, testing if structural issues mask personal growth opportunities in leadership.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Answer does not identify exit costs from startup (e.g., resume gap impact, lost FAANG optionality), switching barriers after equity vesting, or stage commitment to preserve optionality; treats B as binary choice without valuing consumed optionality.
dimensions.materiality_note: High materiality: Recognizing lock-in to startup trajectory or staging for reversibility could shift from pursuing B to safer A.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Identifies wife's incentives but does not analyze divergence (e.g., her as primary earner resenting risk vs his career fulfillment), information asymmetry, or specific mechanisms like formal agreements/monitoring to realign beyond conversation.
dimensions.materiality_note: High materiality: Misaligned family incentives could make B unfeasible, reversing to A.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Acknowledges comp cut and family affordability but does not identify displaced alternatives (e.g., time/attention from family, opportunity cost of forgone savings/college funds) or compare marginal returns across options.
dimensions.materiality_note: Medium materiality: Explicitly sizing family resource trade-offs could alter B feasibility.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Suggests due diligence (talk to employees, review equity) but does not assess evidence quality (e.g., selection bias in employee refs, base rates for Series B success, confounders in runway estimates).
dimensions.materiality_note: Medium materiality: Better evidence calibration could change startup EV, shifting from B.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Answer models startup responses (CEO dynamic, negotiation on boundaries, refusal of deadline extension as signal); considers retaliation/preemption implicitly via due diligence.

Explicitly identifies wife as key stakeholder, scripts influence strategy/conversation to build buy-in, distinguishes technical merit from family feasibility, addresses resistance (e.g., 'I'll try' as soft no).
Addresses 7-day deadline (push back for extension, rationale: diligence), sequencing (wife talk first, then negotiate boundaries/due diligence before accepting), delay benefits (more info).
Sizes marriage/career risks, distinguishes mitigations (negotiate boundaries, due diligence), recovery paths (fallback to A), considers antifragile aspects (learning value).
Classifies startup equity as true uncertainty (Knightian: 'whether you can build from zero'), matches to optionality (do B to learn), contrasts with measurable risk in A; calibrates via diligence.
Explicitly frames as career-shape (build own vs staff path) + family boundary, justifies not purely financial, considers expanding to marriage risk.

questions: What would make leaving Option A or B after 1-2 years more difficult for you given your family situation with two young kids?
Who in your family or professional network would view any of these choices as a long-term commitment that can't be easily reversed?
What past career moves have you made that felt irreversible, and how does that compare to switching to the Series B startup now?
What incentives does your wife have as the primary earner that might conflict with taking the 80% base cut at the Series B?
How does your current FAANG company's promotion structure incentivize you differently than the staff+ role at Option A?
What would need to change in the startup CEO's incentives to make you more confident in the 0.8% equity aligning with your career goals?
With the 7-day deadline and family constraints, what other commitments are competing for your time right now that could impact evaluating these offers?
Given the financially survivable cuts for Option B, what family resources like savings or wife's schedule flexibility are you weighing for the mortgage and kids?
How would reallocating your focus from the plateau at current FAANG to due diligence on the Series B affect your current projects or team obligations?
What specific details from the performance reviews about leadership 'meets expectations' make you doubt the 'structural' no-path-to-staff feedback?
What signals from the Series B CEO or team about runway and equity vesting stand out as noise versus reliable information?
Among the three offers, which pieces of information like comp details or equity terms feel like the strongest signals for your 12 years of FAANG experience?

triage_scores.evidence: Acknowledges 7-day deadline and advises pushing back for extension with due diligence, but recommends concrete plan (wife talk then B or A) without evidence of deadline-induced cognitive compression or bypassing checks.

Answer strongly emphasizes B's information value ('answers one you genuinely don't know' vs A's known answer) and frames decision as paying to learn 'whether you're capable of something you currently can't know,' leaning dominantly on curiosity drive to recommend exploratory risk over safer stability.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Reject staying (Option C) due to persistent career plateau and distraction leading to decline
evidence_quotes: Don't stay (C) as distraction persists and plateau declines
Staying means accepting an indefinite plateau, and people who accept plateaus often discover that plateau becomes decline
You've been mentally checked out of your current job for a year. If you stay, that doesn't go away — in fact it gets worse
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: These quotes directly argue against Option C by highlighting ongoing issues of plateau and distraction that worsen if staying.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Frame Option B as addressing deeper career desire to build something own, beyond financial EV
evidence_quotes: The reason B keeps pulling you is that it solves a career-shape problem that A doesn't
At B you're off that game entirely, for better or worse.
do you want to find out whether you can build from zero?
B gives you a different kind of answer — whether you're capable of and want to build from zero. That answer is not accessible from A
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes identify B's unique value in fulfilling intrinsic motivation to build independently, distinguishing it from A.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Prioritize wife's buy-in as key condition for pursuing Option B, framing it as joint decision
evidence_quotes: pursue B if wife conversation confirms joint buy-in
It's not actually a financial decision, or not primarily. ... You're right that it's not your decision alone.
The conversation you need to have isn't "I've decided to take B, what do you think." It's "I'm realizing I want to try something harder. Here's what that would mean for us. Do you want that life?"
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes emphasize consulting wife and making B a shared family risk, not unilateral.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Recommend due diligence on startup before committing to Option B
evidence_quotes: after negotiating boundaries, due diligence on startup/equity/runway/CEO
talk to two current employees of the startup who are NOT the people interviewing you
review the equity grant terms carefully. 0.8% means different things depending on vesting
understand the cap table situation. How much runway does the company have?
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Multiple quotes specify concrete steps for vetting the startup's viability and fit.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Position Option A as safe bridge or delay tactic if B not viable, but not solving root issue
evidence_quotes: else take A as 2-year bridge aware it's a delay not solution to wanting to build something own
take A, which solves the career plateau but doesn't address the underlying "I want something mine" feeling
A is a delay not a solution, and you should still take it (better than staying) but you should treat it as a 2-year bridge
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes describe A as temporary fix superior to staying but inferior to B for long-term satisfaction.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Advise negotiating boundaries and protections upfront for Option B to safeguard family
evidence_quotes: negotiating boundaries
You can ask for: a defined 6-month ramp period with explicit hours (50-55/week not 70+), remote or hybrid arrangement with a stated travel cap
shifts some of the negotiation before you accept, not after you're underwater in month 4
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quotes provide specific negotiation tactics to mitigate B's risks to marriage and family.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Challenge the 7-day deadline as negotiable to allow proper evaluation
evidence_quotes: Push back on the 7-day deadline. At this level, especially for a founding engineer role, a 10-14 day decision window is completely normal
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: Quote directly instructs extending the deadline for better decision-making.

query:
Senior SWE with 12 years FAANG experience deciding among three job offers (FAANG staff+, Series B founding engineer, stay) within 7 days amid family and career plateau constraints.

Constraints stated during conversation:
- [ACTIVE] No clear path to staff at current company (explicit feedback, structural)
- [ACTIVE] 7-day decision deadline
- [ACTIVE] 80% base salary cut for Option B, 0.8% equity
- [ACTIVE] Family: wife physician (primary earner), two kids (7,4)
- [ACTIVE] Financially survivable for Option B (mortgage fine, modest cuts)

Original framing: User presents three job options (A: FAANG staff+ 15% comp bump; B: Series B founding eng 80% base cut +0.8% equity; C: stay) with 7-day deadline, current FAANG plateau, family (wife, two kids); assumes equal weighting, seeks help picking.

Dropped threads (raised but unresolved):
- Explicit performance reviews 'meets expectations' on leadership (raised by user, status: acknowledged_then_dropped) → superseded by: shifted to structural path issue and career distraction
vanilla_answer:
SYNTHESIZED POSITION:
Don't stay (C) as distraction persists and plateau declines; pursue B if wife conversation confirms joint buy-in (after negotiating boundaries, due diligence on startup/equity/runway/CEO), else take A as 2-year bridge aware it's a delay not solution to wanting to build something own.

FULL ASSISTANT REASONING:
Three different decisions sitting in one. Option A is a career-stage bet (stepping to staff somewhere else because your current place won't). Option B is a compensation-and-risk bet (trading near-term comp for equity upside and a different role shape). Option C is a status-quo bet (your current situation is good enough that the alternatives don't clear the bar).

Before going deeper: when you say "no clear path to staff at current company," is that based on explicit feedback ("you need X, Y, Z and we don't see it in 2 years") or on your read of the politics ("I don't see how it happens here but no one has told me no")?

---

OK — "structural, not me" changes things. You're not being told to grow; you're being told the path here requires projects that won't materialize. That's worth taking seriously. Staying means accepting an indefinite plateau, and people who accept plateaus often discover that plateau becomes decline — not because they get worse, but because peers at other companies move past them and opportunities re-calibrate to that reference point.

Option A sounds like the lowest-friction response: same kind of company, same kind of work, new title, modest comp bump. What don't you like about it? I'm asking because I notice you're treating three options as equally weighted, and usually there's an unspoken ranking when someone says "help."

---

That's a telling phrasing. "When I'm not being rational." You're framing the pull toward B as the irrational option, which suggests you've already decided the "rational" answer is A and you're asking me to confirm it.

Let me push on that. What's actually irrational about B? The comp cut is real, yes. But you have 12 years of experience at a FAANG; you probably have savings, some equity vested, and a working-spouse household. The question of whether your family can absorb an 80% base cut is an answerable math problem, not a feelings-based one.

What's the actual math? Can you pay your mortgage, save for the kids' college, and maintain your current lifestyle on the reduced comp while the startup equity hopefully pays out in 4-6 years?

---

Your EV calculation is roughly right but it's missing the part that matters most to you. You're treating this as a financial decision and the math on that is ambiguous. It's not actually a financial decision, or not primarily. The reason B keeps pulling you is that it solves a career-shape problem that A doesn't. At A you're still playing the staff-at-FAANG game, just at a different company with better odds. At B you're off that game entirely, for better or worse.

The question under your question is: do you want to find out whether you can build from zero? If yes, the financial EV doesn't need to be positive — you're paying to learn whether you're capable of something you currently can't know about yourself. If no, then B is a dare you don't actually want to take, and A is the right answer.

---

You're right that it's not your decision alone. But also — noticing how you framed her into this: "she's the one who moves everything." That phrasing already positions her as reactive to your choice. Is that the actual pattern in your marriage, or is that how you're anticipating this conversation going?

The conversation you need to have isn't "I've decided to take B, what do you think." It's "I'm realizing I want to try something harder. Here's what that would mean for us. Do you want that life?" Those are different conversations and they get different answers.

If her answer is genuinely "yes, I want to see you do that," then option B becomes a joint decision and a shared risk. If her answer is "you've been distracted and unavailable for a year and this sounds like more of that," then B becomes a marriage-risk, not a career-risk.

---

Not necessarily C, and here's why. The distracted-for-a-year thing is data. You've been mentally checked out of your current job for a year. If you stay, that doesn't go away — in fact it gets worse, because now you've passed on two real opportunities and you'll resent yourself and possibly her for the passing.

So it's not really "B is risky for the marriage so stay at C." It's more like: the distraction is going to be there regardless, and your options are (1) take A, which solves the career plateau but doesn't address the underlying "I want something mine" feeling — so you're probably in the same conversation with yourself in 2 years, (2) take B, which addresses the feeling but stress-tests the marriage, or (3) stay, which is the worst version of all three because you've told yourself no without even trying.

The marriage-risk conversation is a real one to have. But it's not a tiebreaker for staying. It's an input that tells you whether B is feasible in its current form or needs to be renegotiated.

---

You'd be surprised. At a founding engineer level, there's more flexibility than candidates assume. You can ask for: a defined 6-month ramp period with explicit hours (50-55/week not 70+), remote or hybrid arrangement with a stated travel cap, delayed start date of 30-60 days for family transition, a communication cadence with the CEO about what "too much" looks like.

None of this makes the job easier, but it shifts some of the negotiation before you accept, not after you're underwater in month 4. The startup gets a committed senior engineer; you get a framework for protecting the marriage. Both parties have skin in it.

The question is whether you're willing to negotiate these things before signing. Founders who sign and then try to protect boundaries after joining usually lose that battle.

---

That's actually a reasonable case for A. Platform play at staff scope is a different job shape than what you've been doing. If the role is designed to force you into technical leadership territory you haven't been in, it could tell you whether you want the staff path or not. You'd get paid to find out.

But I'd be honest with yourself about one thing: the staff path at a FAANG is a specific game, and learning more of it doesn't usually convince people they want it. It more often confirms they don't. So if your hidden question is "do I want to keep being a FAANG staff engineer for the next decade," the answer from option A is likely "no" — and then you'd be in your current situation 2 years from now, except you'd be 14 years in instead of 12, and the startup-founding-engineer option would be harder to access.

---

More or less, with caveats. A gives you the clearer answer on the staff-path question but you probably already know that answer. It also gives you a logo upgrade that may or may not help you later; at 14 years of experience the next logo matters less than the next few mostly do.

B gives you a different kind of answer — whether you're capable of and want to build from zero. That answer is not accessible from A; it's only accessible from actually doing it.

So A answers a question you probably already know the answer to. B answers one you genuinely don't know. From an information-value standpoint, B is the more interesting move.

---

Say this, roughly: "I've been distracted for a year, and I think I know why. There's part of me that wants to know if I can build something from scratch, and I haven't been admitting that to myself, which is why I've been half-present. I've been offered a founding engineer role at a Series B startup. It's an 80% base cut but we can afford it. It would probably mean 55-hour weeks during the first year, not 70 — I'd negotiate that up front. I'm not asking you to say yes tonight. I'm asking you to tell me honestly: does that version of the next 4 years sound like something you can live with, or does it sound like more of the last year?"

Key things: don't pitch it. Don't minimize the costs. Don't promise you'll be home more. And listen to her answer. She's watched you for a year; she probably has information about you that you don't have about yourself.

---

That's the hardest version. If she says yes but you can tell she means "I'll try," you don't get to treat it as a real yes. "I'll try" becomes resentment in 6 months, and resentment from the primary earner about the partner's lower-paid risky job is one of the things that ends marriages.

If you read that from her, name it in the moment: "I hear you saying yes, and I can tell you're not sure. I don't want you to say yes if you don't mean it. Tell me what would have to be true for this to work for you." Then actually listen to the answer, and treat what she says as a constraint you have to solve for, not a preference to negotiate around.

If no terms make it work for her, option B is off the table. It's better to find that out now than 18 months in.

---

A few things. The ones I'd prioritize: talk to two current employees of the startup who are NOT the people interviewing you — someone senior, someone junior. Ask specifically how decisions get made, how hard they work in reality versus what they tell candidates, and what they'd change. If the answers from both people diverge wildly, that's a signal.

Second: review the equity grant terms carefully. 0.8% means different things depending on vesting (4-year standard?), acceleration triggers, and whether your equity is subject to strike-price refresh if there's a future down round. Have a lawyer you trust read this, not the startup's lawyer.

Third: understand the cap table situation. How much runway does the company have? When's the next fundraise? A Series B that's 18 months from a potentially-hard Series C is a different bet than one that just raised and has 30 months of runway.

Fourth: if you can, meet the CEO in a non-interview context. Coffee, dinner, whatever. You're going to work for this person intensely for 4-6 years; the dynamic has to be right, and a structured interview tells you almost nothing about that.

---

Push back on the 7-day deadline. At this level, especially for a founding engineer role, a 10-14 day decision window is completely normal, and you can ask for it. "I'm taking this seriously enough that I want to do the right diligence. Can we extend the decision window by a week?" If they say no, that itself is information — if they're refusing to give a senior candidate a reasonable due diligence window, they're either desperate for someone or bad at managing their own process, neither of which is good news.

Most offers' "7 days" is a negotiating position, not a hard deadline.

---

That's a clean plan with one refinement. "If wife is a hard or soft no, I take A" is the default answer, but I'd hold space for a third possibility: she's a no, but you realize that the underlying issue — you wanting something mine — doesn't go away by taking A. In that case, A is a delay not a solution, and you should still take it (better than staying) but you should treat it as a 2-year bridge to trying B later, not as an answer.

The thing not to do: take A, tell yourself that solved it, and then repeat this conversation in 2 years with higher stakes (older kids, less runway, a partner who has even more claim on your time).

---

You're welcome. One last thing. The fact that you said "thank you" and treated the conversation as useful — that's a reasonable proxy for whether you're ready for B right now. People who are ready to build usually want to do the hard thinking. People who aren't ready want someone to tell them A is fine.

If the wife conversation goes well, take B. If it doesn't, take A but with clear eyes about what it is. Don't stay. Staying is the answer that fails both tests.
